segments.text:
Antigens and Antibodies
Influenza antigen

Antigen
Any molecule that is able to provoke an immune response

Epitope
Specific region of an antigenic molecule that binds to an antibody or a T cell receptor.

Hapten
A hapten is a small molecule that can elicit an immune response only when attached to a large carrier such as a protein; the carrier may be one that also does not elicit an immune response by itself

Adjuvant
An agent which, while not having any specific antigenic effect in itself, may stimulate the immune system, increasing the response to a vaccine
Freund's complete adjuvant is an antigen solution (containing heat killed Mycobacteria) emulsified in mineral oil, used as an immunopotentiator

Antibody
Antibodies (also known as immunoglobulins, abbreviated Ig) are gamma globulin proteins that are found in blood or other bodily fluids of vertebrates, and are used by the immune system to identify and neutralize foreign objects, such as bacteria and viruses

Plasma-fluid part of blood that does not contain cells
Serum-plasma without clotting factors

Serum albumin, a transport protein (lipids) is the most abundant in blood
Globulins are also a heterogenous group of blood proteins
With antibodies
Without antibodies
Serum protein fractionation by electrophoresis

Immunoglobulin
Antibody molecule, consisting of about 20% of the total blood protein by weight

Antibody secretion
Downstream effect of antibody binding

Antibody secretion
~ 2000 antibody molecules/second
Antibodies are bivalent
Lattice can be rapidly phagoctyosed and degraded by macrophages

Antibody Structure
4 Protein Chains
2 Identical Heavy chains (50kd)
2 Identical Light chains (22 kD)
any light chain can associate with any heavy chain, but any antibody molecule will have identical light, and identical heavy chains
Hypervariable regions at antigen binding sites

Papain is a cysteine protease
Fab-fragment with antigen binding activity
Fc- fragment “crystalizable”
Boxes indicate functional units

Immunoglobulin “Fold”
Immunoglobulin Fold. An immunoglobulin domain consists of a pair of β-sheets linked by a disulfide bond and hydrophobic interactions. Three hypervariable loops lie at one end of the structure.
Immunoglobulin superfamily

Light Chains
Kappa  (k)
Lambda (l)

Heavy Chains
M
D
G (4 subclasses)
E
A

Isotype
An antibody class, which is determined by the constant region sequence of the heavy chain.
5 Heavy chain isotypes

IgM is the major class of secreted into the blood in the early stages of a primary antibody response, on first exposure to antigen.
Note J chain: small 159 aa linker protein

IgG
-Most abundant immunoglobulin, ~80%
-secondary immune response
-can cross the placenta
-has 4 subtypes
IgE  binds Fc receptors on mast cells and basophils

IgA the principal class of antibody in secretions including saliva, tears, milk, and respiratory and intestinal secretions.
Same J chain as IgM

IgM
first class of antibody found on B cell membrane
secreted as a pentamer
contains J chain
good at agglutination
important secondary role (complement) in secreted antibodies
IgD
No biological effector function has been identified.
IgG
secondary response
most abundant, making up 80% of serum immunoglobulin
four subclasses
crosses placenta
IgE
binds to Fc receptors on basophils and mast cells, stimulating degranulation of histamine
“E” comes from antigen E of ragweed
IgA
predominant class of secreted antibody
more of this secreted than all other antibodies combined
is trancytosed, like IgM

Transcytosis
The movement of antibody molecules (polymeric IgA or IgM) across epithelial layers mediated by the poly-Ig receptor
